qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Node secret exposed via settings API enables full authentication bypass
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory describes disabled users retaining access because token-based authentication fails to re-check account status and there is no token revocation. This finding is a separate secret disclosure plus header-based authentication bypass (`GET /api/settings` discloses `node.secret`, which is then accepted by `X-Node-Secret`). Different auth mechanism, vulnerable code, and root cause.

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: SSRF via cluster node registration allows authenticated users to probe internal services
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory is an authorization/state-enforcement bug in bearer-token authentication for disabled users. This finding is an SSRF/reverse-proxy issue in cluster node registration and proxying. Different endpoint family, different security property, and different exploit path.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described root cause. GHSA-x234-x5vq-cc2v is specifically about disabled users continuing to use previously issued bearer tokens because token-based authentication does not enforce the disabled-account status check and there is no token revocation. The two findings instead describe (1) disclosure of `node.secret` enabling `X-Node-Secret` authentication bypass and (2) SSRF via cluster node registration. Neither one involves stale bearer tokens, disabled-account enforcement, or the token-validation logic cited by the advisory.
evidence: The advisory points to the login/status-check split in `internal/user/login.go` and `internal/user/user.go`, where login enforces disabled status but token-based authentication does not.

The `node-secret-exposure-via-settings-api` finding instead targets `api/settings/settings.go`, `settings/node.go`, and `internal/middleware/middleware.go`, where disclosing `node.secret` enables a separate header-based auth bypass.

The `ssrf-via-cluster-node-registration` finding targets cluster node creation and reverse proxying (`api/cluster/node.go`, `internal/middleware/proxy.go`), which is unrelated to user-disable enforcement or bearer-token revocation.
